preFix.title: Roast carrots
preFix.subtitle: Whole in butter and thyme until tender and glazed
preFix.excerpt: Whole small carrots or halved large ones roasted in butter with thyme until the edges colour and the natural sugars concentrate. Simple and reliable.

content.text: Roast carrots are a reliable, effort-free side that works alongside almost any roasting joint. The key is hot fat and enough space: crowded carrots steam rather than roast, and the edges will not colour. Cut to similar sizes so they cook evenly.
Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Put the butter and olive oil in the roasting tin and heat for 3 minutes until the butter has melted and is just starting to foam.
Add the carrots and toss to coat. Add the thyme sprigs. Season with salt. Make sure the carrots have enough space to colour rather than steam.
Roast for 30 to 35 minutes, turning once, until tender and the edges are

items.fix: Use two tins if needed. Each carrot should sit in a single layer with a small gap around it.
items.cause: Too many carrots in the tin so they steamed rather than roasted.
items.symptom: The carrots are soft but not coloured

items.fix: Cut halved carrots lengthways to roughly the same thickness. Thicker cuts take longer but caramelise rather than char.
items.cause: Carrots cut too thinly or unevenly.
items.symptom: The outside is charred before the centre is tender

items.fix: Mix the butter with oil, which raises the smoke point. Check at 20 minutes and add a splash of water if the tin looks dry.
items.symptom: The butter has burnt to the base of the tin

items.fix: Season generously. Younger, smaller carrots have a better flavour. A drizzle of honey for the last 5 minutes makes a significant difference.
items.cause: Not enough salt, or the carrots themselves were old and starchy.

content.text: A teaspoon of cumin seeds added to the fat gives a warm spiced flavour. Orange zest scattered over in the last 5 minutes of cooking adds brightness. For a more glazed result, see the honey-roast carrots and parsnips recipe.

Roast carrots are a standard component of the British Sunday lunch table, usually sharing the roasting tin with parsnips or cooked alongside whatever joint is in the oven. The carrot became the most commonly eaten vegetable in Britain in the twentieth century. Roasting is the method most likely to produce a child who claims to like vegetables.
